text: The surgeon encounters adherence of the proximal sigmoid colon in the deep pelvis, likely due to the patient's prior hysterectomy.

The surgeon attempts to free the mesentery to improve visibility for dissection during the robotic rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon attempts to free the mesentery to improve visibility for dissection, manipulating the tissue to gain a better view.
The surgeon continues to unfold the looping of the sigmoid colon, carefully manipulating the tissue to address its folded position within the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon continues to unfold the proximal loop of the sigmoid colon, which is adhered to the rectum and positioned deep within the pelvis.
The surgeon carefully unfolds the proximal loop of the sigmoid colon, which is adhered to the rectum and positioned deep within the pelvis.
The surgeon examines the redundancy of the rectosigmoid mesentery and places it on stretch over the sacral promontory, preparing for fixation.

The surgeon stretches the recto sigmoid mesentery over the sacral promontory, navigating the redundancy to achieve a clear view. The pneumoperitoneum aids in dissecting and revealing the anatomical plane.
The surgeon identifies the hypogastric plexus and nerves, carefully preserving them while performing the posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection.

The surgeon continues to work downwards, manipulating the recto sigmoid mesentery and surrounding tissues to address redundancy and ensure proper positioning over the sacral promontory.
The surgeon continues to work down the surgical field, maneuvering around the iliac vessels on the sidewall, with the right arm occasionally contacting the iliacs during the procedure.

The surgeon navigates the right arm of the instrument, avoiding contact with the iliac vessels on the sidewall. The camera is repositioned from port three to port two, providing a clear view of the sacral promontory. The setup now includes two right ports and one left port, allowing the scissors in arm three to access the deep pelvis without obstruction.

The surgeon port hops the camera from port three to port two, providing a view of the sacral promontory. The setup is adjusted to two right ports and one left port, enhancing the central view of the pelvis and allowing the scissors in arm three to access the deep pelvis without obstruction.
A flap of the bladder is visible in the surgical field. The surgeon uses a nylon Keith needle to suspend the bladder, improving visibility of the anterior cul de sac.
The surgeon identifies a flap of the bladder obstructing the view and suspends it using a nylon Keith needle to improve visualization of the anterior cul de sac.

The surgeon has a clear view of the deep cul de sac and performs anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum. The vagina is visible, and the surgeon palpates both the rectum and vagina to assess the depth of dissection. The surgeon then proceeds with additional lateral dissection.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, reaching the pelvic floor in the posterior, right lateral, and anterior regions, ensuring thorough removal of mesorectal tissue.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, reaching the pelvic floor in the posterior, right lateral, and anterior regions, ensuring thorough removal of the mesorectal tissue.
The surgeon exposes the sacral promontory, ensuring clear visibility of the bone. Bipolar cautery is applied to the vessels directly on the bone to prevent bleeding during needle insertion.
The surgeon exposes the sacral promontory, carefully using bipolar cautery to coagulate vessels directly on the bone, minimizing the risk of bleeding during needle insertion.
The surgeon takes large bites of the periosteum using a silk suture and secures the lateral stalk, demonstrating a technique that avoids the use of mesh.

The surgeon takes large bites of the periosteum using a surgical tool and silk sutures, followed by securing the lateral stalk. The technique appears visually stable despite the absence of mesh placement.
The surgeon takes two bites into the periosteum using a surgical needle, securing the tissue as part of the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal defect to prevent small bowel from descending into the pelvis, using sutures to secure the peritoneal tissue.

The surgeon takes small bites of the peritoneum to close the dissected space, ensuring that the small bowel does not fall into it. Care is taken to avoid proximity to the right ureter and iliac vessels.
The surgeon takes small bites of the peritoneum, exercising caution due to proximity to the right ureter and iliac vessels.
The surgeon sutures the anterior peritoneal flap from the dissected rectovaginal septum to the suture line of the closed peritoneal flap, using robotic assistance for precise manipulation.
The surgeon sutures the anterior peritoneal flap from the dissected rectovaginal septum to the suture line of the closure of the peritoneal flap, using robotic assistance for precision.
